caveats: a: No public occupation or task mix is available specifically for NAICS 533110.
a: Patent licensors and franchisors have materially different legal, commercial, support, and enforcement workflows.
a: Large public-company disclosures may overstate specialization and technology relative to LMM firms, while outside counsel work is not captured by payroll.
rho: No representative source measures AI adoption or realized labor substitution in 533110.
rho: AI may reduce outside professional fees or improve coverage rather than reduce employee labor.
rho: High-stakes legal, valuation, negotiation, and disclosure decisions require review even when drafting is automated.

e: The frozen n value uses a real-estate operations and services margin of 3.96%, which is economically mismatched to potentially high-margin royalty and licensing firms and may materially undercount firms in the target EBITDA band.
e: The NAICS code combines patents, trademarks, brands, and qualifying franchise agreements with different asset lives and operating requirements.
e: Some establishments are passive asset holders or litigation vehicles rather than recurring outsourced operating businesses.
s5: Gallup covers all U.S. employer-business owners and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying control transfers.
s5: IP assets may be sold separately from operating-company control, and internal transfers or portfolio purchases do not qualify.
s5: Institutional, strategic, and passive-entity ownership patterns are not represented by the proxy.
q: No representative source measures automation-benefit retention in 533110.
q: Patent royalty terms can be reset through negotiation, arbitration, courts, or regulation, while franchise royalties depend on franchisee sales and relationships.
q: Large public licensors may have stronger bargaining power and more concentration than LMM firms.
d5: Trademark applications measure potential asset creation, not licensing demand or royalty-bearing activity.
d5: One large patent licensor's agreements and revenues are not representative of all U.S. LMM licensors.
d5: Royalty revenue changes combine price, underlying licensee sales, litigation catch-up, concentration, and quantity.
o: Rights-holder accountability does not guarantee a staffed independent LMM operating company; platforms, pools, law firms, or acquirers can administer assets.
o: Patent pools and standardized licenses are more digitally scalable than bespoke brand and franchise relationships.
o: A passive asset holder may remain legally necessary while falling outside the fixed operating lens.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Lessors of Nonfinancial Intangible Assets (except Copyrighted Works)

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Licensees and franchisees pay fixed fees, minimum guarantees, upfront fees, per-unit or usage fees, and recurring royalties often tied to licensee product sales or franchisee system sales; operating licensors may also receive audit recoveries, renewal or territory fees, and related support or technology fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Copyrighted-work producers and distributors, independent creators, real-property and tangible-asset lessors, franchisors whose classification follows required product or service sales elsewhere, passive one-asset or litigation-only shells without transferable operations, captive internal IP holders, non-control financing situations, and firms outside the target normalized-EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operating licensors that repeatedly assign external rights to patents, trademarks, brand names, and franchise agreements in exchange for royalties or license fees and maintain transferable licensing, administration, compliance, audit, enforcement, or franchise-support operations.

caveats: No public occupation or task mix is available specifically for NAICS 533110.
Patent licensors and franchisors have materially different legal, commercial, support, and enforcement workflows.
Large public-company disclosures may overstate specialization and technology relative to LMM firms, while outside counsel work is not captured by payroll.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-and-time study from independent patent, trademark, brand, and franchise licensors, distinguishing already automated systems and external legal spend from employee work, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Contract review, licensee discovery, portfolio mapping, disclosure drafting, royalty-report ingestion, audit selection, invoice and renewal administration, research, correspondence, brand monitoring, franchise support, and document production are exposed to AI assistance. Rights strategy, valuation, negotiation, relationship management, legal judgment, enforcement, standards participation, brand governance, and accountable approvals remain expert-intensive.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: USPTO reports trademark filings up 7% through midyear fiscal 2026, while InterDigital reports eight patent license agreements entered in 2025 and recurring multi-year royalty contracts. Transformation: treated continued rights creation and licensing activity as directional evidence for modest five-year real demand growth, then widened the range for asset expiration, validity, franchise health, renewal, litigation, and concentration. Error direction: filings and a large licensor's deal flow can overstate LMM licensing demand, while expanding technology and brand commercialization could make the low case conservative.
caveats: Trademark applications measure potential asset creation, not licensing demand or royalty-bearing activity.
One large patent licensor's agreements and revenues are not representative of all U.S. LMM licensors.
Royalty revenue changes combine price, underlying licensee sales, litigation catch-up, concentration, and quantity.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-price series of active third-party license agreements, royalty-bearing units or sales, franchise agreements, renewals, terminations, and licensee counts for establishments classified in 533110 would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Trademark filings were 7% higher through the first half of fiscal 2026 than the comparable 2025 period, and patent licensors continue signing multi-year agreements, indicating continuing creation and commercial use of protected intangible assets. Five-year demand can fall with asset expiration, invalidity, weak franchise economics, insourcing, open standards, litigation outcomes, or licensee concentration, while new brands, technologies, and business formats support growth.

caveats: The frozen n value uses a real-estate operations and services margin of 3.96%, which is economically mismatched to potentially high-margin royalty and licensing firms and may materially undercount firms in the target EBITDA band.
The NAICS code combines patents, trademarks, brands, and qualifying franchise agreements with different asset lives and operating requirements.
Some establishments are passive asset holders or litigation vehicles rather than recurring outsourced operating businesses.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level dataset covering ownership, active license count, royalty revenue, normalized EBITDA, contract duration, renewal and concentration, chain of title, remaining asset life, disputes, franchise-unit trends, employee and outside-counsel functions, and shell status would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Recurring royalty contracts and franchise networks can be transferable, but eligibility is reduced by passive or single-asset entities, disputed or expiring rights, customer concentration, key-person negotiation, litigation dependence, weak chain of title, portfolio obsolescence, unfavorable franchise relations, limited operating infrastructure, and economics outside the target band. The frozen firm count is especially uncertain because it applies a 3.96% real-estate-services margin to intangible-asset lessors that may have much higher margins.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 533110 ($3,255,724,774) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 533110 ($51,548,751,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0396 (margins.json[53]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Real Estate (Operations & Services) EBITDA/Sales 3.96% (agency/brokerage/property-management economics, dominant in firm counts)); revenue/employee $1,304,074 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 533110); class <5: 1,430 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,534,550 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $139,968 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 300 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,586,103 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $142,010 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 217 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,651,770 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $184,210 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 212 firms, avg revenue/firm $25,050,019 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $991,981 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 81 firms, avg revenue/firm $43,475,506 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,721,630 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 127 firms, avg revenue/firm $280,133,535 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,093,288 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 81

o.bridge: Source population/quantity: official NAICS licensing scope, FTC franchisor disclosure obligations, and public examples of patent and franchise licensing, royalty audits, negotiation, enforcement, support, and recurring contracts. Transformation: assigned high operator need to title, contracting, disclosures, negotiation, brand or portfolio maintenance, royalty verification, franchise support, disputes, and enforcement, while discounting for standardized licenses, pools, automated marketplaces, direct licensing, and passive administration. Error direction: current legal and relationship processes may overstate staffing needs as platforms mature, while rights ownership, enforceability, disclosure, and negotiation keep accountable-operator need high.
caveats: Rights-holder accountability does not guarantee a staffed independent LMM operating company; platforms, pools, law firms, or acquirers can administer assets.
Patent pools and standardized licenses are more digitally scalable than bespoke brand and franchise relationships.
A passive asset holder may remain legally necessary while falling outside the fixed operating lens.
o.change_evidence: License and franchise cohorts comparing traditional licensors with automated marketplaces, pools, self-service platforms, and direct owner-to-user licensing, including renewal, leakage, disputes, enforcement, franchise support, and realized royalties, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: AI and licensing platforms can automate discovery, matching, drafting, reporting, monitoring, and administration, and passive direct licensing can reduce staffing. The current basket still generally requires an accountable rights holder or operating licensor to establish title, set terms, make disclosures, negotiate, grant permissions, maintain portfolios and brands, audit royalties, support franchise systems, resolve disputes, and enforce rights.

caveats: No representative source measures automation-benefit retention in 533110.
Patent royalty terms can be reset through negotiation, arbitration, courts, or regulation, while franchise royalties depend on franchisee sales and relationships.
Large public licensors may have stronger bargaining power and more concentration than LMM firms.
q.change_evidence: Matched licensing and franchise contract, renewal, concession, legal-spend, audit-recovery, staffing, support-cost, and contribution-margin data before and after automation would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Multi-year fixed-fee, minimum-guarantee, usage, and sales-based royalty contracts can let licensors retain workflow savings until renewal, while asset-light operations support high incremental retention. Capture is reduced by licensee and franchisee bargaining, renewals, concessions, audit recoveries shared through negotiation, litigation and enforcement cost, court or regulatory rate setting, disclosure obligations, support expectations, and royalties that fall with underlying licensee sales.

caveats: No representative source measures AI adoption or realized labor substitution in 533110.
AI may reduce outside professional fees or improve coverage rather than reduce employee labor.
High-stakes legal, valuation, negotiation, and disclosure decisions require review even when drafting is automated.
rho.change_evidence: Audited pre/post evidence from licensing and franchise organizations covering hours, outside-counsel spend, royalty leakage, contract cycle time, renewal outcomes, disclosure errors, disputes, and enforcement results would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years permits adoption of contract copilots, portfolio and prior-art search, royalty ingestion, anomaly detection, renewal workflows, disclosure drafting, licensee support, and brand monitoring. Realization is constrained by privilege and confidentiality, hallucination risk, bespoke agreements, data fragmentation, legal accountability, uncertain ownership and validity, negotiation strategy, court and regulator scrutiny, and the need for human approval of enforcement and franchise representations.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: Gallup's fall-2024 survey reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners plan to sell, transfer, or take public within five years. Transformation: used 22% as an upper anchor and discounted for noncompletion, family or internal transfers, asset-only transactions, passive entities, chain-of-title and validity diligence, litigation, licensee concentration, consent and renewal risk, and key-person dependence. Error direction: broad owner intentions overstate completed qualifying control transfers, while strategic demand for established brands or patent portfolios could make the lower bound conservative.
caveats: Gallup covers all U.S. employer-business owners and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying control transfers.
IP assets may be sold separately from operating-company control, and internal transfers or portfolio purchases do not qualify.
Institutional, strategic, and passive-entity ownership patterns are not represented by the proxy.
s5.change_evidence: Closed control-sale counts and eligible-firm denominators from IP transaction advisers, patent and trademark assignment records linked to corporate control changes, franchise M&A databases, and industry brokers over a five-year cohort would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners report meaningful five-year sale or transfer intentions, but qualifying control transfers of licensors require completed deals, transferable and enforceable rights, licensee and franchisee continuity, manageable concentration, clean chain of title, acceptable litigation, and retention of key legal and commercial capabilities.

principal_driver: The principal driver is recurring royalty revenue supported by AI-assisted contract, reporting, audit, monitoring, renewal, and licensee-support workflows.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is an uncertain and potentially miscounted transferable-firm pool built on rights whose value can be concentrated, disputed, expiring, passive, or dependent on a few licensees.
